creator: Julie Bort
title: (No AI) Alphabet’s record-breaking $85B raise for Google’s AI business is a helluva good signal
dc:creator: Julie Bort
content: If Alphabet's record-breaking $85 billion stock sale signals investor appetite for AI-related offerings, we can see that investors are ready to chow.
contentSnippet: If Alphabet's record-breaking $85 billion stock sale signals investor appetite for AI-related offerings, we can see that investors are ready to chow.

creator: Lucas Ropek
title: (No AI) Google’s Dreambeans, its weirdest-named AI tool to date, will turn your life into a cartoon
dc:creator: Lucas Ropek
content: Dreambeans is a curated list of AI-illustrated "stories" culled from the personal data in your Google account.
contentSnippet: Dreambeans is a curated list of AI-illustrated "stories" culled from the personal data in your Google account.

title: (No AI) As AI gets better, it reveals an empty promise
author: TC. Sottek
content:
![](https://platform.theverge.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/chorus/uploads/chorus_asset/file/11503853/mdoying_180419_1777_0454_2.jpg?quality=90&strip=all&crop=0,0,100,100)

This week we've got tandem hands-ons with Google's new Gemini AI agent - Spark - from my colleagues [David Pierce](https://www.theverge.com/ai-artificial-intelligence/941388/gemini-spark-ai-agent-trip-planning) and [Jay Peters](https://www.theverge.com/tech/941138/google-gemini-spark-ai-agent-hands-on). Their takeaways are similar: It's so effective that it's scary. Spark knew that David's dog is named Frida and knew the first name of Jay's wife, even though neither of them explicitly provided this information to Google. But what's scary to _me_ is how all of this stuff seems geared toward a future of "productivity" that completely misses what needs to be fixed in our world.

"Productivity" is often pitched as a panacea for what befalls us in our personal lives, even going so far as to implicate our moral worthiness …

[Read the full story at The Verge.](https://www.theverge.com/ai-artificial-intelligence/942629/as-ai-gets-better-it-reveals-an-empty-promise)

Spark knew that David's dog is named Frida and knew the first name of Jay's wife, even though neither of them explicitly provided this information to Google. But what's scary to me is how all of this stuff seems geared toward a future of "productivity" that completely misses what needs to be fixed in our world.
"Productivity" is often pitched as a panacea for what befalls us in our personal lives, even going so far as to implicate our moral worthiness …
Read the full story at The Verge.

title: (No AI) Amazon’s search bar will invent AI-generated products you can’t buy
author: Emma Roth
content:
![An image showing AI-generated Amazon results](https://platform.theverge.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/2026/06/amazon-ai-search.png?quality=90&strip=all&crop=0,0,100,100)

Amazon's updated search bar [will now show you AI-generated images](https://www.aboutamazon.com/news/retail/visual-search-shopping-features) of products as you describe them. For now, the in-app feature only surfaces AI images of clothing and home goods, allowing you to tap on the image that best matches what you're looking for and search for similar-looking items.

In a blog post, Amazon positions the feature as a way to help you search for items if you can't remember the name of a specific texture or style, like describing a "shirt with a draped collar" if you can't think of "cowl neck." The feature seems like it might come in handy in these kinds of scenarios, but it doesn't really add much if you're just searc …

[Read the full story at The Verge.](https://www.theverge.com/tech/942547/amazon-search-bar-ai-images)

title: (No AI) xAI Asks Court to Strip Alleged Grok Deepfake Nudes Victims of Anonymity
